title: ElasticMM: Efficient Multimodal LLMs Serving with Elastic Multimodal Parallelism

reviews.summary: The paper proposes ElasticMM, a serving system for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) that introduces Elastic Multimodal Parallelism (EMP). EMP separates requests into modality-specific groups (text-only vs. multimodal), disaggregates inference into encoding, prefill, and decode stages, and elastically reallocates resources across groups and stages via gain-cost–guided preemption. Two additional optimizations—Unified Multimodal Prefix Cache and Non-blocking Encoding—aim to reduce redundant computation and inter-stage interference. On a single 8×A800 node, ElasticMM reports up to 4.2× lower TTFT and 3.2–4.5× higher throughput than vLLM and up to 2.3× over a DistServe-style disaggregated baseline under two real-world mixed workloads.
strengths: The modality-aware decoupling (separate text-only and multimodal groups) is a clear, pragmatic design choice that addresses the heterogeneous resource footprints of mixed traffic.
The stage-level elastic partition scheduling with gain–cost models for preemption across prefill/decode provides a systematic handle to adapt parallelism to phase-specific bottlenecks.
Unified caching that integrates multimodal token reuse with textual prefix reuse is a sensible, composable optimization that aligns with observed redundancy in production traces.
Non-blocking encoding is an effective system-level decoupling that reduces prefill interference and improves TTFT for image-heavy workloads.

Evaluation includes both encoder–decoder and decoder-only MLLMs (LLaMA3.2-Vision-11B, Qwen2.5-VL-7B) and two mixed real-world datasets with complementary characteristics (long text vs. high-resolution images).
Ablations isolate the impact of EMP versus static allocations and quantify the incremental benefit of each optimization (cache vs. non-blocking encoding).
Throughput is measured under varying SLO regimes, exploring strict and relaxed settings.
The system decomposition into modality-level and stage-level scheduling is presented cleanly, with intuitive diagrams that anchor the reader.
The paper clearly motivates the heterogeneous nature of MLLM serving and the poor scalability of decode, justifying stage-specific elasticity.
The problem—cost-efficient, SLO-compliant serving for mixed text/multimodal traffic—is timely and important for both academic and industrial deployments.
The results, although single-node, show sizable improvements and highlight practical levers (modality isolation, stage elasticity, caching) that are likely to generalize.
weaknesses: The approach relies on intra-node migration with fast interconnects; migration overheads (KV/image cache size, transfer paths) are not quantified, and sensitivity to weaker interconnects is not studied.
The gain–cost models are presented at a high level without sufficient algorithmic detail (e.g., how T(·) and L(·) are estimated online, stability/oscillation controls, penalty calibration).
The unified multimodal prefix cache raises correctness and privacy questions (e.g., cross-user deduplication policies, image hash collisions, cache invalidation across model/version changes).

Missing comparisons to the most relevant recent MLLM serving systems that also decouple E/P/D and provide dynamic scheduling (e.g., EPD Disaggregation, HydraInfer) and to KV-centric disaggregated frameworks with caching (MemServe). This omission weakens the novelty and claims.

The evaluation is restricted to a single node with 8×A800 and NVLink; there is no study on multi-node, commodity Ethernet, or PCIe-only environments where elasticity and transfers can behave differently.
DistServe is extended with static allocation only; it is unclear if stronger baselines (e.g., chunked-prefill, more advanced routing, or recent vLLM/SGLang advances) were enabled uniformly.

Normalized “input latency (s/token)” is used as a proxy for TTFT in several plots; this conflation makes it hard to assess absolute TTFT SLO conformance and complicates head-to-head baseline interpretation.
Some formulas/equations include prose and lack precise definitions of terms, making reproduction difficult.

Recent and highly related systems are not empirically compared: EPD Disaggregation (Encode–Prefill–Decode), HydraInfer’s hybrid EPD topologies and stage-aware batching, MemServe’s KV-first disaggregated caching, DuetServe’s intra-GPU phase isolation, and EcoServe’s partial disaggregation on commodity interconnects. While some are mentioned, the lack of comparisons leaves open whether gains are due to the specific scheduling or to general EPD disaggregation principles.
reviews.questions:
1. How are T(R,E), L(B,E), and M(e) estimated online in the gain–cost models? What profiling windows and smoothing/hysteresis are used to avoid oscillations or thrashing when workloads shift quickly?
2. What are the exact mechanisms and data paths for KV and image-token migration (CUDA IPC, NCCL P2P, CPU DRAM staging)? Can you report average and P95 migration times and transferred sizes under your workloads?
3. How do you prevent cross-user or cross-tenant data leakage in the unified multimodal prefix cache? Is caching scoped per-session or per-tenant? How are model/version changes handled (cache invalidation) and how are image hashes constructed to avoid collisions or adversarial cache poisoning?
4. Could you provide absolute TTFT/TBT SLO thresholds and SLO attainment (P95/P99) in addition to “normalized latency per token”? This would make it easier to compare with prior work and production SLOs.
5. How would ElasticMM behave on commodity interconnects (PCIe/Ethernet) or multi-node clusters? Can you share a sensitivity study or projections for migration overheads and scheduling decisions under weaker links?
6. Why are HydraInfer/EPD Disaggregation and MemServe not included as baselines? If they were unavailable, can you discuss expected performance differences or provide a controlled comparison (e.g., reusing your hardware/testbed) in a revision?
7. For encoder–decoder models with cross-attention, do you isolate batches by modality at the LLM backend in all cases? Any cases where mixed batches are still formed and how do you avoid the known inefficiencies there?
reviews.assessment:
ElasticMM tackles an important and timely problem: efficient, SLO-compliant serving for mixed multimodal and text-only traffic. The paper’s two-tier separation (modality groups plus stage disaggregation) is conceptually clean, and the elastic scheduling guided by gain–cost heuristics is a practical addition that plausibly drives the reported gains. The unified multimodal prefix cache and non-blocking encoding are straightforward yet effective optimizations, and the evaluation shows substantial improvements over monolithic vLLM and a static DistServe-style baseline across two representative MLLMs and two real workloads.

However, the paper omits empirical comparisons to the most closely related recent systems that also decouple E/P/D and provide dynamic scheduling/migration (EPD Disaggregation, HydraInfer) or KV-first disaggregated caching (MemServe). The evaluation is single-node on high-bandwidth GPUs, with limited visibility into migration overheads, tail latencies, and sensitivity to interconnect quality. The scheduling models lack detail on online estimation and stability. These gaps make it difficult to assess how much of the benefit stems from the modality-aware separation versus broadly adopting EPD disaggregation principles known in the literature. Overall, I view the work as promising and practically useful, but currently closer to a strong systems engineering contribution than a clearly distinguished research advance relative to recent EPD/hybrid frameworks. I recommend a weak reject with the caveat that adding strong baselines, tail/SLO analyses, and sensitivity studies could elevate the work to a solid accept.
